task_description: Search for and select a one-way budget flight from Los Angeles to San Francisco and choose the basic fare option.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in crop shows the top navigation bar of the United Airlines website. A semitransparent red X is centered on the rounded dark-blue button labeled "Booking" with a small downward chevron, indicating the exact click target. This is a top-area navigation control, not a form field or canvas object. In the full-screen view, the browser is open to the United Airlines homepage, with the main booking panel below and the same top navigation bar across the page.
caption.think: The user likely wants to open the Booking navigation menu or switch focus to booking-related options from the site header.
caption.action: Click the "Booking" dropdown button in the top navigation bar.
caption.expectation: The Booking menu should open or the booking section should become active, showing related navigation options.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in crop shows a white dropdown menu panel under the United Airlines header. A semitransparent red X is centered on the underlined text link labeled "Shop by map" in the "Book a flight" column, indicating the exact click target. This is a UI control within an open navigation menu, not a blank area or canvas object. In the full-screen view, the United Airlines homepage is open in Chrome, with the top navigation expanded from the previously selected "Booking" menu and multiple travel-related links visible across the dropdown.
caption.think: The user likely wants to open the map-based flight shopping option from the Booking menu.
caption.action: Click the "Shop by map" link in the open Booking dropdown menu.
caption.expectation: The site should navigate to the shop-by-map page or load a map-based flight search interface, and the dropdown menu should close or transition to the new page.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in crop shows the right side of the white-outlined "Flying from" input field. The text inside reads "Hong Kong HKG," and the semitransparent red X is centered within this input box, indicating the exact target is the form field itself, which is a UI control rather than the map canvas or a blank region. In the full-screen view, the United Airlines interactive route map page is open in Chrome under the heading "Explore destinations." A red warning banner says no results were found, and the search controls row includes "Flying from," "Search type" set to "Roundtrip," "Dates" set to "Flexible dates," a max price field, and a blue "Search" button above the map.
caption.think: After reaching the route map page and seeing a no-results message, the user likely wants to activate the origin field to edit or replace the departure airport.
caption.action: Click inside the "Flying from" input field containing "Hong Kong HKG."
caption.expectation: The origin field should become active with a text cursor or selected text, allowing the departure location to be edited or replaced, and possibly showing location suggestions.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in view is effectively the same as the full screen and shows the active "Flying from" text entry field outlined in blue. Inside that field, the existing origin text has been replaced and currently shows "LA," indicating text is being entered into the departure-airport field rather than clicking another control. In the full-screen context, this is the United Airlines "Explore destinations" interactive map page in Chrome, with a red warning banner stating no results were found, nearby controls for "Search type" set to "Roundtrip," "Dates" set to "Flexible dates," a max price field showing "$2000," a blue "Search" button, and a North America map below.
caption.think: The user is editing the origin airport after the prior no-results message, typing a new departure code to change the search from Hong Kong to Los Angeles.
caption.action: Type "LAX" into the active "Flying from" field, replacing the previous origin text.
caption.expectation: The field should display "LAX" or an airport suggestion matching Los Angeles, and the origin search should be ready for the next selection or search step.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in crop shows the autocomplete suggestion list opened beneath the active "Flying from" field. A semitransparent red X is centered on the first suggestion, which is highlighted with a dark outline and reads "Los Angeles, CA, US (LAX)." This target is a UI control within the dropdown list, not the map or a blank area. In the full-screen view, the United Airlines "Explore destinations" page is open in Chrome with the origin field containing "LAX," a red warning banner above, search controls for roundtrip and flexible dates, a blue "Search" button, and a map below.
caption.think: After typing LAX, the user wants to choose the matching airport entry from the suggestion list so the departure airport is set correctly before searching again.
caption.action: Click the "Los Angeles, CA, US (LAX)" suggestion in the dropdown under the "Flying from" field.
caption.expectation: The Los Angeles suggestion should be selected, the dropdown list should close, and the origin field should be set to the chosen airport, ready for the next search step.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in crop shows the open "Search type" dropdown on the United Airlines page. A semitransparent red X is centered on the top dropdown field that currently displays "Roundtrip" with a downward chevron, indicating the target is this UI control rather than one of the list items below. The dropdown menu beneath it shows two options: "Roundtrip" highlighted in blue and "One Way" beneath it. In the full-screen view, Chrome is displaying the "Explore destinations" page with "Flying from" set to "Los Angeles LAX," a red warning banner about no results found, the "Dates" field set to "Flexible dates," a blue "Search" button, and a large map below.
caption.think: The user is interacting with the search type selector and likely wants to dismiss or toggle the currently open dropdown before choosing a different search configuration or continuing.
caption.action: Click the "Search type" dropdown field showing "Roundtrip."
caption.expectation: The search type control should respond by toggling the dropdown state, most likely collapsing the open menu while keeping "Roundtrip" selected.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in crop shows the open "Search type" dropdown on the United Airlines page. A semitransparent red X is centered directly over the second dropdown option labeled "One Way," which is highlighted in blue, indicating the exact target is that menu item in a UI control list rather than the map or a blank area. In the full-screen view, Chrome is open to the "Explore destinations" page with "Flying from" set to "Los Angeles LAX," a red warning banner about no results found, the "Dates" field showing "Flexible dates," a max price field, a blue "Search" button, and a North America map below.
caption.think: The user likely wants to change the trip search from roundtrip to one-way so the route search uses a one-way itinerary.
caption.action: Click the "One Way" option in the open "Search type" dropdown.
caption.expectation: The dropdown menu should close and the "Search type" field should update from "Roundtrip" to "One Way," indicating the trip mode has been changed.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in crop shows the semitransparent red X centered inside the "Dates" input field, directly over the text "Flexible dates" and left of the small blue calendar icon. This is a UI control in the search form, outlined in blue to indicate focus. In the full-screen view, Google Chrome is open to United Airlines' "Explore destinations" page. The search bar row shows "Flying from" set to "Los Angeles LAX," "Search type" set to "One Way," the "Dates" field showing "Flexible dates," a max price field, and a blue "Search" button, with a map of North America below and a red warning banner above.
caption.think: After setting the trip type to one-way, the user likely wants to change or open the date selection control to refine the destination search.
caption.action: Click the "Dates" field showing "Flexible dates".
caption.expectation: The dates control should become active and open a date-selection menu or panel for choosing travel dates or date flexibility options.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in crop shows a date-selection popup beneath the Dates field. A semitransparent red X is centered on the tab labeled "Exact Dates," which has a dark outline around it. To its right is another tab labeled "Flexible Dates" with a blue underline indicating it is currently active. In the full-screen view, Google Chrome is open to United Airlines' Explore destinations page. The search form shows "Flying from" set to "Los Angeles LAX," "Search type" set to "One Way," the Dates field still visible above, a max price field, and a blue Search button. The popup is open over the map area, so the target is a UI control in the popup, not the map.
caption.think: The user likely wants to switch the date mode from flexible dates to specific calendar dates for the one-way destination search.
caption.action: Click the "Exact Dates" tab in the date-selection popup.
caption.expectation: The popup should switch from the flexible-dates view to the exact-dates view, making "Exact Dates" the active tab and showing controls for selecting specific travel dates.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in crop shows a calendar date cell in the April 2026 month view. A semitransparent red X is centered on the dark-blue highlighted square labeled "1," which sits under the weekday header "We," indicating Wednesday, April 1. In the full-screen view, Google Chrome is open to United Airlines' Explore destinations page. The date picker popup is open in "Exact Dates" mode above the map, with April 2026 and May 2026 calendars visible, the Dates field currently showing "Departure," and the target is a date cell inside the calendar popup, which is a UI control rather than the map or a blank area.
caption.think: The user has switched to exact dates and now wants to choose a specific departure date, starting by selecting April 1, 2026.
caption.action: Click the date cell "1" in the April 2026 calendar.
caption.expectation: That date should become selected as the departure date, updating the calendar selection and likely filling the Dates field with April 1, 2026 or advancing the date picker to the next step.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The zoomed-in crop shows the semitransparent red X centered inside the "Max price" input box, directly over the value "$2000." The field has a blue outline, small up/down spinner arrows on the right inside the box, and a blue sliders-style filter icon at the far right edge of the same control. In the full-screen view, Google Chrome is on United Airlines' Explore destinations page with the search row showing "Flying from" set to "Los Angeles LAX," "Search type" set to "One Way," "Dates" set to "Apr 01," and the map below. The target is a UI control in the top search form, specifically the max-price numeric input field.
caption.think: After selecting the departure date, the user likely wants to adjust the budget filter by activating the max price field so it can be edited or changed.
caption.action: Click the "Max price" input field showing "$2000."
caption.expectation: The max-price field should become active for editing, placing focus in the input and allowing the value to be changed, with the field remaining selected or showing a text cursor.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The visible text entry area is the focused "Max price" input box in the search form. It has a blue outline, small up/down spinner arrows on the right inside the field, and a blue sliders-style filter icon at the far right edge of the same control. The field currently shows "$000," indicating the previous "$2000" value has been replaced and the digit being entered is forming a new maximum price value. In the full-screen view, Chrome is on United Airlines' Explore destinations page with "Flying from" set to "Los Angeles LAX," "Search type" set to "One Way," "Dates" set to "Apr 01," a blue "Search" button beside the price field, a red warning banner saying no results were found, and a map of North America below.
caption.think: The user is editing the max-price filter to a much lower budget, entering the first digit of a new value after activating the field.
caption.action: Type "1" into the focused "Max price" field, replacing the prior value rather than clicking anywhere else.
caption.expectation: The max-price field should update to reflect the newly typed digit as the start of a new price value, with the field remaining focused for additional typing or adjustment.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The cropped image shows a large dark-blue rounded rectangular button labeled "Search," with a semitransparent red X centered directly on the button text. Immediately to its left is a small white square button containing a blue sliders/filter icon. In the full-screen view, this is on the United Airlines "Explore destinations" page in Chrome, within the top search form row showing "Flying from" set to "Los Angeles LAX," "Search type" set to "One Way," "Dates" set to "Apr 01," and "Max price" showing "$1000." A red warning banner above says no results have been found. The target is a UI control in the form area, not the map.
caption.think: The user has finished lowering the maximum price and now wants to run the updated destination search using the current filters.
caption.action: Click the dark-blue "Search" button in the search form.
caption.expectation: The search should submit immediately, refreshing the results or map based on the current filters and possibly updating the warning or destination results shown.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The cropped image shows a semitransparent red X placed on a plain light-gray/white rectangular area directly below the search filters and just above the map, not on a button, field, icon, price tag, or map marker. The nearby visible labels above are "Search type" with "One Way." In the full-screen view, this blank region is part of the top-left overlay above the interactive United Airlines destination map, near the "List of search results" panel area. This is a click on a blank region rather than a specific UI control, canvas object, or handle.
caption.think: The user likely wants to dismiss focus from the search controls or simply click an empty area of the interface to continue without selecting any map result or filter control.
caption.action: Click the blank light-colored area above the map and below the search form.
caption.expectation: No major visible change should occur beyond possible loss of focus from any previously active input; no menu or dialog should open, and no object should become selected.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The cropped view shows the full browser window with no semitransparent red X, indicating a scroll action rather than a click. The page is the United Airlines "Explore destinations" screen, with the search controls at the top, an expanded left panel titled "List of search results" showing destinations such as Puerto Vallarta, San José del Cabo, Las Vegas, and Honolulu with prices, and a large map of the United States on the right filled with blue destination dots and white price tags. In the full-screen context, a cookie consent banner spans the bottom of the page, and the results list appears to be the most likely scrollable content area currently in focus.
caption.think: The user likely wants to move upward within the currently focused content after viewing lower items in the search results, most likely scrolling the destination results list back toward earlier entries near the top.
caption.action: Scroll up within the destination results area on the left side of the page.
caption.expectation: The visible content should shift upward, revealing earlier items higher in the results list or moving the page slightly upward if the page itself has focus.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The cropped image shows a semitransparent red X centered on the rounded white price/date pill beneath the destination entry "San Francisco, CA, US (SFO)." The pill has a black outline and blue text reading "Wed, Apr 1 from $188," with a right-facing chevron at the far right, indicating it is a selectable result control rather than blank space. In the full-screen context, this control is inside the left-side "List of search results" panel on the United Airlines "Explore destinations" page, beside a large destination map on the right. This is a click on a list item UI/object within the results panel, not on the map or a toolbar.
caption.think: The user likely wants to select the San Francisco search result from the results list to view or focus that destination, likely syncing the selection with the map or opening destination-specific details.
caption.action: Click the rounded white "Wed, Apr 1 from $188" result pill under "San Francisco, CA, US (SFO)."
caption.expectation: The San Francisco result should become selected, and the interface should immediately update by highlighting that destination and/or showing related details or map focus for San Francisco.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The cropped view shows the full browser window with no semitransparent red X, indicating a scroll action rather than a click. The page is now on the United Airlines flight results screen rather than the earlier destination map list. Visible near the top are search fields for From "LAX," To "SFO," Dates "Apr 01," Travelers "1 Adult," a "Show price in" dropdown set to "Money," and an outlined "Update" button. Below that are filter chips including "Sort," "Stops," "Fare Type," "Duration," "Connections," "Refundable fares," "Aircraft," and "Wheelchair." The main content area shows "DEPART ON: April 1" with fare columns such as "United Economy," "Economy Plus," and "United First," and the first visible result row begins with "NONSTOP." In the full-screen context, a cookie consent banner stretches across the bottom, and the page content appears vertically scrollable.
caption.think: The user likely wants to move farther down the flight results to see more itinerary options and fare rows below the currently visible top section of results.
caption.action: Scroll down on the flight results page.
caption.expectation: The page should move downward, revealing lower portions of the available flight listings and additional itinerary rows beneath the currently visible results.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The cropped view shows a semitransparent red X centered on the text-and-arrow control labeled "Select flight →" inside a light blue fare card showing "From $188." This is a selectable UI control within the flight results grid, not blank space or a toolbar item. In the full-screen context, the page is the United Airlines flight results screen for LAX to SFO under "DEPART ON: April 1," with columns for "United Economy," "Economy Plus," and "United First." The red X is on the top visible "United Economy" fare tile for the first nonstop itinerary row.
caption.think: The user likely wants to choose the $188 United Economy option for the first listed nonstop flight and proceed to the next booking step or fare details for that itinerary.
caption.action: Click the "Select flight →" control inside the light blue "United Economy" fare card showing "From $188" for the first nonstop flight row.
caption.expectation: That fare option should become selected, and the site should immediately open the next step with flight/fare details or booking options for the chosen itinerary.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The cropped view shows the full browser window with no semitransparent red X, indicating a scroll action. The United Airlines booking page is displaying a fare-selection area for the chosen nonstop LAX to SFO flight. At the top of the visible content are the flight details with departure and arrival times and fare columns labeled "United Economy," "Economy Plus," and "United First." Below, a large light-blue panel titled "Select a fare to continue" is open, showing fare cards including "Basic (Most restrictive)" for $188, "Standard" for $223, and "Flexible (Fully refundable)" for $263. In the full-screen context, a cookie consent banner still spans the bottom of the page, and the page content extends below the currently visible fare cards, indicating the main webpage is the scrollable region.
caption.think: After opening the fare-selection panel, the user likely wants to move farther down to see more of the available fare details or controls within this selection section before choosing an option.
caption.action: Scroll down on the main United Airlines webpage.
caption.expectation: The page should move downward, revealing lower portions of the fare-selection panel and any additional options or continuation controls below the currently visible fare cards.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The cropped view shows a semitransparent red X centered on the small empty square checkbox to the left of the text "Basic Economy works for me." near the bottom of the leftmost fare card. The card is labeled "United Economy Basic (Most restrictive)" and lists restrictions such as no upgrades, no changes allowed, checked bag fees, and no carry-on bags. In the full-screen context, this is the United Airlines fare-selection overlay titled "Select a fare to continue," with three fare columns: Basic for $188 on the left, Standard for $223 in the middle, and Flexible for $263 on the right. The target is a UI control inside the fare card, not blank space.
caption.think: The user likely needs to acknowledge the Basic Economy restrictions by checking the confirmation box so the disabled gray "Select" button for the Basic fare can become available.
caption.action: Click the empty checkbox next to "Basic Economy works for me." in the leftmost Basic fare card.
caption.expectation: The checkbox should become checked, indicating selection, and the Basic fare's gray "Select" button should become enabled or otherwise ready for the next click.

caption.observation: Cropped image shows The cropped image shows a semitransparent red X centered on the rounded blue button labeled "Select" at the bottom of the leftmost fare card. Above it, the checkbox next to "Basic Economy works for me." is already checked. This target is a clickable UI control inside the fare card, not a blank region. In the full-screen context, the page is a United Airlines fare-selection overlay titled "Select a fare to continue," with three columns: "Basic (Most restrictive)" for $188 on the left, "Standard" for $223 in the middle, and "Flexible (Fully refundable)" for $263 on the right. The clicked control is the enabled Select button for the Basic fare.
caption.think: After acknowledging the Basic Economy restrictions, the user now intends to confirm that fare choice and proceed with the booking flow using the lowest-priced Basic option.
caption.action: Click the blue "Select" button in the leftmost "Basic (Most restrictive)" fare card.
caption.expectation: The Basic fare should be selected, and the site should advance to the next booking step or load the next page for the chosen itinerary.
